title: One-Hot Encoding Limits
concept: One-hot encoding represents words as sparse, high-dimensional vectors that fail to capture any semantic relationships (e.g. 'cat' and 'dog' look as different as 'cat' and 'spoon').

exercises.prompt: What is the basic idea behind 'one-hot encoding' in text processing?

options.text: Representing words as simple binary lists with a single '1'
Translating words into different languages automatically
Measuring the temperature of computer processors

exercises.explain: One-hot encoding represents words as lists of zeros and ones where only one position is active (1), hence the name 'one-hot'.

cards.title: Do Words Have Neighbors?
cards.body: If you tell a human that a 'golden retriever' is a type of dog, they instantly know it shares traits with a 'poodle'. But to a simple computer program using basic lists, the words 'retriever', 'poodle', and 'calculator' are all just random, unrelated labels. Let's see why!

cards.title: What is One-Hot Encoding?
cards.body: One-hot encoding is a method where each word in a vocabulary is represented by a long list of zeros with a single '1' at a unique position. Because every word gets its own isolated '1', the system cannot detect any shared meanings or relationships between different words.

cards.title: The Box Analogy
cards.body: Imagine writing down every word in the dictionary on separate sticky notes and putting them in their own separate cardboard boxes. A box labeled 'puppy' and a box labeled 'dog' are completely isolated. To a computer using one-hot encoding, these boxes look just as unrelated as 'dog' and 'airplane' because there are no connecting threads between the boxes.

cards.title: A Simple Dictionary
cards.body:
If our dictionary only has three words: ['cat', 'dog', 'mug'], their one-hot vectors look like this:

• cat: [1, 0, 0]
• dog: [0, 1, 0]
• mug: [0, 0, 1]

Mathematically, 'cat' and 'dog' look exactly as different from each other as 'cat' and 'mug' do. There is no 'animalness' shared between them.

cards.title: The Math Myth
cards.body: Some people think that because computers are great at math, they can easily see that 'warm' and 'hot' are similar from their one-hot codes. But in reality, one-hot vectors are completely independent. The math sees them as completely perpendicular, meaning zero similarity is captured.

cards.title: Isolated vs. Connected
cards.body: Compare how one-hot encoding views words versus how humans (and advanced AI models) view them.

right.text: Sees 'cat' and 'dog' as close neighbors because they are both furry pets.

left.text: Sees 'cat' [1,0,0] and 'dog' [0,1,0] as 100% unrelated and perpendicular.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following is true about one-hot encoded word vectors?

options.text: They are short lists filled with decimal numbers.
They are sparse, meaning they contain mostly zeros and only a single one.
They change values depending on the sentence context.
They use negative numbers to represent verbs.

exercises.explain: In one-hot encoding, every word gets a vector of the same size as the vocabulary, with only one '1' and all other positions set to '0'. This makes the vectors very long and mostly empty (sparse).

exercises.prompt: True or False: One-hot encoding requires larger and larger vectors as your vocabulary grows.
exercises.explain: Because every word gets its own unique dimension, a vocabulary of 50,000 words requires vectors that are 50,000 numbers long. This is highly inefficient and takes up massive amounts of memory.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe the limitation of one-hot encoding.
exercises.sentence: One-hot encoding represents words as isolated vectors, which fails to capture any semantic relationships between them.
exercises.explain: One-hot encoding only flags the presence of a word (using a 1) but does not record how that word relates to other words, leaving it blind to semantic connections.

exercises.prompt: Two systems represent words. System A groups 'happy' and 'joyful' close together. System B gives 'happy' [1, 0, 0] and 'joyful' [0, 1, 0]. Which system represents one-hot encoding?

options.text: System A, because it groups synonyms together.
System B, because it treats all words as unrelated, isolated points.
System C, because it uses complex sentences to define words.

exercises.explain: AI-based word embeddings are designed to place similar words close together in vector space. One-hot encoding, however, treats all words as completely independent and equally different, showing no AI-like understanding of meaning.

exercises.prompt: Why does one-hot encoding fail to help AI models understand that 'cat' and 'kitten' are related?

options.text: It automatically groups similar words together in a list.
It treats every word as entirely separate, capturing zero similarity.
It uses negative numbers to show words that mean the opposite.
It represents words as tiny, dense packets of overlapping meanings.

exercises.explain: One-hot encoding represents every single word as a unique, independent direction (dimension) in space. Because all these directions are at right angles (orthogonal) to each other, the math shows they are all equally different. 'Cat' is mathematically just as far from 'kitten' as it is from 'computer'.
